title: The evolution of sepsis care: from protocol-driven management to personalized intensive care
one_line_summary: Sepsis management is evolving from rigid protocols to a personalized, phenotype-guided model, integrating new guidelines (DSG 2025, SSC 2026) with a focus on perfusion-oriented resuscitation and formalizing refractory septic shock.
key_pearls: Sepsis care is shifting from rigid protocols to a personalized, phenotype-guided model, emphasizing dynamic adjustments after initial stabilization.
Peripheral vasopressor administration is now supported for early stabilization to avoid delays associated with central venous access.
Capillary Refill Time (CRT) is a key bedside marker for tissue perfusion, alongside lactate clearance, and should be prioritized over static MAP targets.
SSC 2026 recommends risk-stratified antimicrobial timing: 1-hour for high-probability, 3-hour for possible, and diagnostic-driven for low-probability sepsis.

Refractory septic shock is a formalized entity, defined by persistent hypoperfusion despite initial resuscitation and high vasopressor needs (> 0.5 µg/kg/min norepinephrine equivalents), guiding therapeutic escalation.
Routine use of adjunctive therapies like vitamin C, immunoglobulins, blood purification, and probiotics is not advised due to lack of evidence.

sections.heading: Guideline Evolution
sections.content: Sepsis guidelines are evolving from rigid protocols, such as SSC 2021, towards more flexible decision-support frameworks like the German S3 guideline (DSG 2025) and the Surviving Sepsis Campaign (SSC 2026). DSG 2025 offers operational, practice-oriented guidance, while SSC 2026 adopts a broader, system-oriented continuum of care approach.

sections.heading: Early Recognition
sections.content: The approach to early sepsis recognition is moving away from qSOFA as a standalone diagnostic tool. There is an increased emphasis on multimodal, continuous screening methods, including tools like NEWS2, MEWS, and SIRS criteria.

sections.heading: Hemodynamic Management
sections.content: Hemodynamic management is shifting from a pressure-oriented approach (e.g., MAP >= 65 mmHg) to a perfusion-oriented resuscitation strategy. Peripheral vasopressor administration is now supported for early stabilization to avoid delays associated with central venous access. Capillary Refill Time (CRT) is highlighted as a key bedside marker of tissue perfusion, though it requires methodological standardization. The SSC 2026 guideline suggests age-adapted MAP targets, specifically 60-65 mmHg for patients over 65 years.

sections.heading: Antimicrobial Strategy
sections.content: The SSC 2026 guideline introduces risk-stratified timing for antimicrobial initiation: 1-hour for high-probability infection, 3-hour for possible sepsis, and diagnostic-driven approaches for low-probability cases. There is strong support for prolonged or continuous beta-lactam infusion after an initial loading dose. Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (TDM) is recommended for critically ill patients who exhibit high pharmacokinetic variability.

sections.heading: Refractory Septic Shock
sections.content: Refractory septic shock is now formally defined as persistent hemodynamic instability and tissue hypoperfusion despite adequate initial resuscitation. Criteria include persistently elevated lactate, prolonged Capillary Refill Time (CRT), lack of fluid responsiveness, and high vasopressor requirements, specifically norepinephrine equivalents greater than 0.5 µg/kg/min. This entity serves as an action-guiding classification for therapeutic escalation, such as the consideration of adjunctive therapies or advanced hemodynamic monitoring.

sections.heading: Adjunctive Therapies
sections.content: Both the DSG 2025 and SSC 2026 guidelines advise against the routine use of various adjunctive therapies, including vitamin C, immunoglobulins, blood purification techniques, and probiotics, citing a lack of sufficient evidence to support their widespread application in sepsis management.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.content: The DSG 2025 guideline uniquely incorporates a dedicated chapter on vaccination as a core sepsis prevention strategy. This includes recommendations for vaccinations against influenza, COVID-19, pneumococci, and other relevant pathogens.

strengths_limitations: The transition to personalized sepsis care is supported by emerging trials (ImmunoSep, ANDROMEDA-SHOCK-2, TIGRIS). However, clinical implementation faces challenges due to the need for standardized definitions of perfusion markers and a lack of robust outcome data for TDM-guided dosing.
